urn:contentItem:6JJP-XYY3-S4CC-F2J4-00000-00PILLSBURY WINTHROP SHAW PITTMAN LLP - INDUSTRY NEWS: INSIGHTS FROM PERKINS COIE AND OTHERS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:37ZLexisNexisSTOCKPILLSBURY WINTHROP SHAW PITTMAN LLP - INDUSTRY NEWS: INSIGHTS FROM PERKINS COIE AND OTHERS

DASHBOARD: PILLSBURY WINTHROP SHAW PITTMAN LLP

PermID4296383614Websitewww.pillsburylaw.com/en/IndustryFull-Service BigLawAddress31 West 52nd Street, New York, NY 10019 USA

ACTIVITIES:

Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP, founded in 1868, is a global law firm headquartered in New York City. With over 700 lawyers across 20 offices, it serves clients in energy, technology, and finance, specializing in M&A, regulatory, and litigation. Generating over $700 million annually, Pillsbury advised on 2024 renewable energy transactions, recognized by Columbia Law School. Its culture emphasizes innovation, diversity, and pro bono work, with strong associate training programs. Competing with Baker McKenzie, Pillsbury stands out for its energy and tech regulatory expertise. In 2024, it expanded its AI and ESG practices, focusing on sustainable finance. Recent initiatives include cloud-based legal tech and partnerships with nonprofits like Lawyers Alliance for New York, reinforcing its community impact.

+ PILLSBURY WINTHROP SHAW PITTMAN LLP [1 News That Matters]

+ MORGAN LEWIS EXPANDS BENEFITS TEAM WITH PILLSBURY HIRE

Morgan Lewis & Bockius LLP has announced the addition of a prominent attorney from Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP to its Employee Benefits team, a strategic step to address the growing complexities in benefits law and the demand for expert guidance.The attorney's multi-faceted background covers compliance and regulatory issues within employee benefits, and is expected to broaden the firm's capabilities in advising clients on benefit plans and pension laws.Arriving on 08th April 2026, the hire aligns with Morgan Lewis' broader push to strengthen its offerings in employee benefits amid regulatory shifts and an increasingly competitive legal market.Pillsbury has seen several of its attorneys move to other firms in recent years, and Morgan Lewis notes that the new hire will bring important regulatory insight to navigate evolving requirements.The firm has a longstanding reputation for depth in its practice areas, and leaders say the addition will help deliver more specialized services tailored to client needs.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ PERKINS COIE [3 News That Matters]

+ ASHURST-PERKINS COIE MERGER CLEARED BY PARTNERS, CLOSE TARGET SET FOR Q3

Partner votes have backed the Ashurst and Perkins Coie merger, with the approval announced on 13th April 2026 and a closing target in the third quarter, subject to customary conditions.The move underscores a shared commitment to building a forward looking global firm and confidence in growth and in delivering premier legal services in a fast changing market.Paul Jenkins, CEO of Ashurst, said the two firms are well aligned and their combination will broaden client offerings and scale cross border capability.The combined group will number about 3,000 lawyers across 52 offices in 32 countries, with major hubs in Seattle, London, Sydney and New York, and a focus on technology, energy, infrastructure and financial services.The practice areas, together with a revenue base of around USD 2.8 billion, bring extensive international experience spanning Brussels, Dubai, Frankfurt, Hong Kong, Paris, Seoul, Shenzhen, Singapore and Tokyo. Leadership will be shared by Bill Malley and Paul Jenkins as global co-CEOs, with Ashurst global chair Karen Davies and Perkins Coie corporate practice chair Brian Eiting serving as co-chairs.The combination is expected to sharpen the firms' competitive edge and position the new entity for sustained growth in the evolving global legal market.

+ ASHURST AND PERKINS COIE AGREE TO STRATEGIC COMBINATION, LAUNCH ASHURST PERKINS COIE

NEW YORK — Perkins Coie and Ashurst announced today that the partnerships of both firms have voted overwhelmingly to approve their proposed combination, signaling broad support for a deal that pairs complementary geographic reach, sector depth, and practice strength. The date of this announcement is recorded as 2026-04-14 16:18:02.950901.Upon closing, Ashurst Perkins Coie will be a globally integrated firm with combined revenues of approximately US$2.8 billion and around 3,000 lawyers spread across more than 50 offices worldwide. The firm will run flagship hubs in Seattle, London, Sydney, and New York, underscoring its expansive footprint across key markets. It will be well positioned to serve clients in technology, energy and infrastructure, and financial services.Bill Malley, the Firmwide Managing Partner at Perkins Coie, described the vote as recognition that the merger will create a differentiated platform with scale, sector depth, and technology leadership to meet cross-border client needs. 'Our partnership's approval reflects a belief that combining to form Ashurst Perkins Coie will yield a differentiated global platform.'Paul Jenkins, Global CEO of Ashurst, stressed the alignment and ambition behind the move, noting that the combination will unlock complementary strengths across sectors and practice areas that will benefit clients. 'This vote confirms our joint ambition and the compelling platform it creates for top legal talent,' he said.The merger remains subject to customary closing conditions and is expected to finalize in the third quarter of 2026, with the new firm aiming to navigate the evolving global market and deliver enhanced services to clients.

+ PERKINS COIE RE-ESTABLISHES MAINLAND CHINA PRESENCE WITH SHENZHEN HUB

Perkins Coie is re-entering China with a Shenzhen representative office, inaugurated on Apr. 14, 2026, marking a renewed foothold in the market. The move follows the 2024 closure of its Beijing and Shanghai offices and signals a strategic pivot toward Shenzhen to tap the city's rapid growth as a tech and manufacturing hub. The office will be led by Bing Ai, chief representative and managing partner, and Wei Yuan, representative and litigation partner; both are US-trained and bring deep experience serving technology clients. Ai focuses on patent procurement and portfolio management in sectors such as semiconductors and medical devices, while Yuan handles patent infringement disputes. Bill Malley, Perkins Coie's managing partner, says the Shenzhen move underscores the firm's commitment to cross-border US legal expertise and helping clients navigate global markets amid rising cross-border challenges. The development comes as several international firms push deeper into Shenzhen's Greater Bay Area, and Perkins Coie weighs a potential merger with UK-based Ashurst that could create a top-20 global firm with projected annual revenues around $2.7 billion.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ COOLEY LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ CROSBY RAISES $60 MILLION IN SERIES B LED BY LUX CAPITAL AND INDEX VENTURES, WITH COOLEY AMONG BACKERS

Crosby, the contract review start-up, announced it has closed a $60 million Series B on April 3, 2026, its third capital raise in under a year, with Cooley LLP among the backers.The round was co-led by Lux Capital and Index Ventures, with participation from Sequoia, 01 Advisors, Bain Capital Ventures and angel investor Elad Gil.The fresh capital will fund product development and help Crosby scale to serve a growing roster of clients, including Clay, Rogo and Ramp.Crosby uses artificial intelligence paired with legal expertise to accelerate contract reviews, and the company says the value of negotiated contracts now tops $1 billion, up from roughly $30 million when it emerged from stealth last year.In a blog post, Crosby stated, "As we’ve scaled, we’ve also started experimenting with entirely new technology experiences." The company has rolled out simulations of counterparty responses to contract redlines, voice agents that negotiate on clients’ behalf, and a client-facing platform to oversee Crosby’s work.Crosby was founded in 2024 by Ryan Daniels and John Sarihan. Daniels, a former Cooley associate, and Sarihan, a former Google software engineer and Ramp tech lead, have steered Crosby toward a future of faster contract negotiation with the support of investors including Cooley and other leading firms.

+ COOLEY TAPS FRESHFIELDS BRUCKHAUS DERINGER ALUMNI TO BOOST TECH PRACTICE

Cooley LLP is expanding its technology practice by recruiting several attorneys who previously worked at Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer, signaling a strategic effort to strengthen its tech capabilities and stay competitive in a dynamic legal market.As of 23rd November 2025, the firm’s latest recruitment drive highlights ongoing work to broaden technology offerings in a rapidly evolving sector. The addition of Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer alumni is anticipated to bolster the existing practice and help attract clients seeking specialized tech law guidance.The incoming team members bring broad experience, advising notable clients on IP, data protection, and corporate tech transactions. Their know-how is expected to augment Cooley’s well-established venture capital and startup advisory practice, further positioning the firm as a leading tech law player.The hire reflects a broader market trend toward poaching top talent to meet demands of a fast-paced, tech-driven environment. With tech-related legal challenges growing more complex, specialized knowledge is increasingly vital, making these hires timely.Through this expansion, Cooley seeks to reinforce its standing as a go-to tech law firm, supporting its innovation-driven growth. As integration proceeds, stakeholders will watch how these changes affect Cooley's market position and client services in the near term.

+ COOLEY LLP'S LITIGATION TEAM NAVIGATES A LEADERSHIP TEST AMID GROWTH

Cooley LLP’s litigation practice has been making headlines as a finalist for The American Lawyer's General Litigation Department of the Year. The firm, noted for its excellence, is currently confronting what partners describe as a 'test of leadership.' The practice, led by Ian Shapiro, is attending to critical challenges amid a changing legal landscape.The discussion about the firm's strategic direction occurred during a recent interview with Ian Shapiro, along with partners Michael Attanasio and Kathleen Hartnett, where they outlined the firm’s decision-making framework. They highlighted Cooley's representation of Jenner & Block in its high-profile bid against the administration, illustrating a commitment to impactful legal work. The interview appeared just 10 hours prior, providing a timely window into the firm's operations.In addition to Jenner & Block matters, Cooley has pursued a range of pro bono matters that reflect its devotion to public service and social justice. This focus has contributed to the firm's growth during the past year. As the litigation department grows, the firm is positioned to leverage its expertise in tackling complex legal challenges while remaining aligned with its core values.The growth of Cooley's litigation practice aligns with broader industry trends toward specialized services. This pivot enhances the firm's competitive edge and bodes well for future opportunities. As the fiscal year nears its end, stakeholders will closely monitor Cooley's next steps.As the 'test of leadership' plays out, the legal community and investors will be watching to see how Cooley navigates these challenges. The firm's proactive stance, together with a pursuit of excellence, suggests it may emerge stronger in a competitive market.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ REED SMITH LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ REED SMITH EXPANDS LA PRIVATE EQUITY TEAM WITH SENIOR PARTNER APPOINTMENT

ReedSmith LLP has expanded its private equity practice with the appointment of a new partner in its Los Angeles office, a move aimed at strengthening service to investment firms and corporate clients in mergers and acquisitions.The partner joins on 4th May 2026 and brings substantial experience in private equity transactions and related advisory services, supporting the firm's effort to navigate complex deals.The Los Angeles market continues to attract capital inflows into private equity, and the firm expects to leverage the new partner's expertise to tailor solutions for its clients.The addition reinforces Reed Smith's reputation for high-quality legal services and long-term client relationships as the private equity landscape evolves and demands more sophisticated guidance on investment structures and regulatory matters.The firm is well-positioned to capitalize on growth opportunities in private equity with this hire.

+ REED SMITH APPOINTS LONGTIME PHILADELPHIA EXECUTIVE TO LEAD LOCAL PRACTICE

ReedSmith LLP has named an internal partner to lead its Philadelphia office, underscoring a continuity-driven transition in a key market. The appointee has spent more than ten years at the firm and has played a pivotal role in high-profile cases, helping shape strategy for the Philadelphia footprint. The decision to promote from within aligns with the firm's strategy to cultivate internal talent while ensuring leadership continuity. The Philadelphia office is a core part of Reed Smith's national network, serving clients across sectors, and the new leader will prioritize strengthening core practice areas and expanding the firm's local footprint. Effective from 27th April 2026, the move signals optimism for future growth as the firm seeks to deepen client relationships and drive operational excellence in Philadelphia.

+ REED SMITH NAMES BOSTON THE SITE OF ITS INAUGURAL MANAGING PARTNER ROLE, SIGNALING GROWTH

ReedSmith LLP has appointed its first Managing Partner in Boston, signaling a new era for the firm in the Northeast market. Effective from 23rd April 2026, the appointment designates a local leader to head Boston operations and advance client development. The role is designed to strengthen relationships with current clients while pursuing new business opportunities across sectors where Boston has shown momentum. The firm noted that the decision follows an analysis of the region's burgeoning legal market and Reed Smith's broader expansion strategy in key markets. By tapping into local insights and forming partnerships aligned with its global vision, Reed Smith aims to embed itself more deeply into Boston's business landscape. The new Managing Partner brings a wealth of experience and a wide professional network that executives expect will raise operational efficiency and bolster the firm's reputation in the area. The Boston office is set to be a central pillar of the firm's growth plan as Reed Smith navigates the evolving legal market, with investors and stakeholders watching closely in the coming months.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ WILSON SONSINI GOODRICH & ROSATI P.C. [3 News That Matters]

+ ARCELLX-GILEAD MERGER GUIDED BY WILSON SONSINI AS DEAL CLOSES

Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati P.C. advised Arcellx in its acquisition by Gilead Sciences, with the transaction completed on 29th April 2026, underscoring the firm’s central role in life sciences M&A. Serving as lead adviser, the firm drew on its deep public-company M&A experience and a long relationship with Arcellx, guiding the company from its IPO to strategic collaborations, including the Kite Pharma agreement. The deal strengthens Arcellx’s development program by pairing its pipeline with Gilead’s resources, and the Wilson Sonsini team—consisting of Robert T. Ishii, Ross J. Tanaka, and Dan R. Koeppen—navigated governance, regulatory and capital markets considerations to support a smooth closing.

+ AVOCA SECURES $125 MILLION FUNDING ROUND LED BY MERITECH AND GENERAL CATALYST

Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati P.C. has advised Avoca as the company closes more than $125 million in financing across Seed, Series A and Series B rounds, announced on 27th April 2026, valuing Avoca at about $1 billion. The Series B round was led by Meritech and General Catalyst, while Kleiner Perkins participated in the Series A. Proceeds are earmarked to expand Avoca's 24/7 call handling, scheduling and the development of tailored marketing campaigns and customer follow-ups. The Wilson Sonsini team advising Avoca on the transaction included Rob Broderick, Sam Obenhaus, Estefano Martinez and Ayush Sahni, reflecting the firm's depth in capital markets and emerging companies. Avoca's platform is designed to streamline operations for service businesses, boosting efficiency and responsiveness. The new funding is expected to accelerate product development and broaden the platform's reach in service automation. Stakeholders are urged to review Avoca's official news release for more details.

+ WILSON SONSINI APPOINTS BAKER MCKENZIE VETERAN AS CHIEF OPERATING OFFICER

Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati P.C. has named a new Chief Operating Officer from Baker McKenzie, underscoring the firm's focus on strengthening operations. The identity of the incoming COO remains confidential for now, with the hire aimed at boosting efficiency and supporting growth in a competitive legal market.The announcement dated 22-04-2026 signals a strategic leadership update as Wilson Sonsini navigates industry changes and invests in technology and service excellence. The firm expects the new COO's experience in technology integration and client management to help streamline processes and enhance service delivery to clients.Wilson Sonsini's track record of technology focus and innovation aligns with the incoming executive's background in overseeing operational strategies at a large global firm. The leadership profile is seen as well suited to expanding the firm's presence in biotechnology, cybersecurity and intellectual property work.Market watchers welcomed the appointment, viewing it as a move to strengthen strategic direction and execution. Analysts say the hire could influence the firm's competitive position and future performance as it scales.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ SIDLEY AUSTIN LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ TELIX-REGENERON RADIOPHARMACEUTICAL PACT VALUED UP TO USD 2.1 BILLION; SIDLEY AUSTIN ADVISES TELIX

Sidley Austin is advising Telix Pharmaceuticals on its strategic collaboration with Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, a partnership valued at up to USD 2.1 billion. The arrangement will see Telix and Regeneron co-developing and co-commercialising next-generation radiopharmaceutical therapies under a 50:50 cost-and-profit-sharing model, with Telix receiving USD 40 million upfront for four initial programmes. Disclosed on 21st April 2026, the deal centers on joint governance and shared development and commercialization responsibilities, aiming to leverage both companies' oncology platforms. Telix is pursuing an aggressive expansion in radiopharmaceuticals, seeking to broaden its footprint in cancer care through partnerships that combine Regeneron's oncology capabilities with Telix's radiopharmaceutical pipeline. Joshua Hofheimer, a Sidley partner, commented that "These considerations affect governance, regulatory matters, and intellectual property, in particular." The alliance is positioned to accelerate radiopharmaceutical development while balancing regulatory and commercial considerations. Industry observers note that the deal aligns with a broader wave of M&A activity in the biopharma space, and Sidley Austin continues to build its reputation as a premier adviser on cross-border collaborations in a changing regulatory environment.

+ SIDLEY AUSTIN EXPANDS CITY FOOTPRINT AS REVENUE RISES 33% ON HIRING DRIVE

Sidley Austin is reporting a 33% increase in City revenue, credited to a targeted hiring push as of 14th April 2026. The growth underscores Sidley's expanded City footprint following recruitment of senior lawyers and other specialists. Executives say the hires have diversified practice areas and improved client service, lifting billable hours and retention. Leadership intends to refine hiring practices and broaden capabilities to stay ahead in a competitive market. Industry observers see the firm's approach as a potential model for other firms navigating tighter margins and evolving client needs.

+ CFTC TAPS SIDLEY AND LATHAM TO SHAPE FORWARD-LOOKING RULES FOR DIGITAL DERIVATIVES

The CFTC has established an Innovation Task Force by engaging SidleyAustin and Latham & Watkins to advance the regulatory framework for the derivatives markets, aligning oversight with rapid technological change.The agency announced on 11th April 2026 that Sidley Austin, with deep regulatory experience, and Latham & Watkins, providing strategic counsel, will help translate market developments into robust policy.Sidley Austin's team is expected to offer guidance on best practices as the derivatives space integrates technologies such as blockchain and artificial intelligence.The task force forms part of a broader CFTC strategy to both regulate and facilitate innovation, seeking to balance risk management with opportunities for market participants to deploy new tools.Industry stakeholders will watch for potential policy shifts and how the collaboration could influence regulatory expectations, with implications for U.S. competitiveness in global markets.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG, LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG COUNSELS GOLDEN ENTERTAINMENT IN PRIVATIZATION BY FAMILY STAKEHOLDERS

Greenberg Traurig, LLP represented Blake L. Sartini in the successful transition of Golden Entertainment to a private, family-owned operation, with closing finalized on 30th April 2026. The move marks a milestone for Sartini, a respected figure in the gaming industry.As part of the deal, shareholders received a cash dividend of $2.75 and 0.902 shares of VICI Properties Inc. for each Golden share, a structure designed to provide immediate liquidity while preserving exposure to a major gaming and hospitality platform.VICI Properties acquired the real estate assets tied to seven Golden Entertainment casino resorts, and a triple-net master lease was established with Sartini-controlled entities, allowing Golden to continue operating its casino and tavern portfolio without interruption.Co-Managing Shareholder Michael J. Bonner of Greenberg Traurig's Las Vegas office commented, "We were honored to represent Blake L. Sartini in returning Golden Entertainment to private family ownership. We congratulate Blake and the entire Golden Entertainment team!" The firm highlighted its proficiency in corporate transactions and real estate, noting the collaboration of a team including Dmitriy A. Tartakovskiy, Jim Mace, and James Doyle, among others.With about 3,100 lawyers across 51 locations globally, Greenberg Traurig continues to be recognized for its innovative legal services and strategic solutions in the corporate and real estate space.

+ TOKYO OFFICE HOSTS ANREV BRIEFING ON REAL ASSETS, DATA CENTRES AND AI OPPORTUNITIES

Greenberg Traurig, LLP's Tokyo office hosted a briefing for ANREV on Real Assets & New Technologies: Data Centres & AI Opportunities on the 30th of April, 2026. The panel included Greenberg Traurig Shareholder Joel H. Rothstein, who chairs the Asia Real Estate Practice, as well as other experts. The session explored the transformative potential of artificial intelligence for capital allocation, infrastructure planning, and investment strategies across global real asset markets, with particular emphasis on the surge in data centre activity in the Asia-Pacific region driven by rapid digitalization. Rothstein highlighted Greenberg Traurig's commitment to fostering industry dialogue about trends shaping real estate and infrastructure. He stated, 'These conversations are increasingly important as investors and developers navigate the rapid convergence of digital infrastructure, real estate, and emerging technologies.' The firm leverages its cross-border capabilities to support complex, multi-jurisdictional investments in digital infrastructure and other real assets. Greenberg Traurig, with roughly 3,100 lawyers across 51 locations, is known for its innovative approach to real estate and infrastructure law. The firm has earned recognition as a 2025 BTI Best of the Best Recommended Law Firm and consistently appears on Am Law Global 100 and Law360 400 lists.

+ MELTONES DELIVER VICTORY FOR CHARITABLE CAUSES AT LAW ROCKS LOS ANGELES

On 29th April 2026, Greenberg Traurig, LLP's house band The Meltones emerged victorious at Law Rocks Los Angeles, staged at Whisky A Go Go in West Hollywood, as donations and sponsorships topped the night’s haul to exceed $65,000, with $41,000 designated for The Headstrong Project.The Law Rocks event, spanning three nights, set a regional record by raising more than $569,000—the most for Los Angeles in 15 years. The Meltones opened with a Green Day anthem, and the outcome was decided by a mix of fan contributions, ticket sales, and judges’ scores. Greenberg Traurig also served as a Hall of Fame sponsor.The Meltones’ roster blends partners and staff across real estate, government policy, and litigation, including Stephen L. Rabinowitz and Burt Bruton on guitar, Christopher A. Cernik on keyboard, and vocalists Michael R. Hogue, Ashley A. LeBlanc, and Tracy Newirth; guest Tom Helf (drums) and Daniel Blumberg (bass) complemented the lineup. The group, named for co-founder Mel Greenberg, has long been a fixture of GT’s culture and has continued to spotlight the firm’s community efforts, including a recent Law Rocks NYC win raising over $100,000 for The Door. Greenberg Traurig now operates with about 3,100 lawyers in 51 offices worldwide.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ GOODWIN PROCTER LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ SIDLEY AND GOODWIN STEER ESPERION'S $1.1B TAKE-PRIVATE IN BIOTECH SECTOR SHIFT

Esperion Therapeutics Inc. has entered a $1.1 billion take-private agreement, with Sidley Austin LLP and Goodwin Procter LLP handling the transaction's legal work. The deal, announced on 1st May 2026, is designed to reposition the lipid-management specialist away from public market volatility and toward a deeper focus on its programs.The arrangement comes amid growing investor and private equity interest in biotechnology, with private ownership seen as a way to streamline operations and accelerate development.Sidley and Goodwin are recognized for their mergers-and-acquisitions expertise and have been tasked with guiding the process through governance and regulatory considerations.Industry analysts say moving to private ownership could enable longer-term planning and increased investment in R&D, with investor attention turning to Esperion's performance after the transition.The deal also spotlights the pivotal role of major law firms in facilitating large biotech transactions, potentially signaling more private-market activity in the sector.

+ FBT GIBBONS ADDS VENTURE CAPITAL ATTORNEY FROM GOODWIN PROCTER LLP

On 21st April 2026, FBT Gibbons confirmed the recruitment of a venture capital attorney from Goodwin Procter LLP to its New Jersey office. The hire strengthens the firm's venture capital practice as the sector grows. The incoming attorney brings broad experience from Goodwin Procter LLP, known for its robust venture capital work spanning startups and established companies. FBT Gibbons says the addition will help clients raise capital and navigate complex investment environments. The move reflects a wider industry trend of firms expanding niche capabilities to serve clients in New Jersey and beyond.

+ TOPSPIN CONSUMER PARTNERS CLOSES $328 MILLION FUND WITH LEGAL SUPPORT FROM GOODWIN PROCTER

Goodwin Procter LLP supported Topspin Consumer Partners in closing its latest fundraising effort, securing $328 million for a consumer-focused fund. The achievement marks a notable development in the private equity landscape, with Goodwin Procter providing essential counsel throughout the process.On April 15, 2026, Topspin announced the fund’s close, signaling an intent to capitalize on opportunities within the consumer sector. The firm’s legal team helped navigate complex fundraising dynamics, ensure regulatory compliance, and streamline commitments from potential investors.The fund will pursue a wide range of opportunities across consumer markets, with a focus on sectors showing robust growth. Through Goodwin Procter’s support, Topspin Consumer Partners seeks to identify and invest in companies well-positioned to perform in a changing market environment.The collaboration highlights Goodwin Procter’s reputation in private equity and its ongoing commitment to helping clients achieve their investment objectives, potentially boosting investor confidence as the fund scales.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ ORRICK, HERRINGTON & SUTCLIFFE LLP [3 News That Matters]

+ ORRICK ADDS EXPERIENCED USPTO VETERAN TO EXPAND IP PRACTICE

Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe has expanded its IP practice by hiring a senior attorney from the USPTO, a move that reflects the firm's ongoing push into technology and telecommunications sectors.The new attorney will officially join on the 28th of April, 2026, bringing a wealth of experience from the USPTO, including patent examination and litigation.The hire is expected to augment Orrick's ability to guide clients through patent strategy and protection in rapidly changing markets.Orrick's managing partner commented that the addition reinforces the firm's commitment to best-in-class IP services, with professionals who understand patent law's intricacies.Industry observers note that the firm's ability to attract talent from leading institutions could influence its growth trajectory and competitive position in technology-driven sectors.

+ ORRICK BOLSTERS DIGITAL SAFETY TEAM WITH META VETERAN

On Apr. 23, 2026, Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe announced the appointment of the former Meta youth safety policy head, adding depth to its digital security and regulatory practice.The incoming advisor brings a robust background in policy formulation and implementation, with specific focus on safeguarding young users in digital environments.Officials say the hire will help Orrick advise clients on a broad set of issues, including privacy protections, data privacy regulations, and compliance obligations tied to online youth safety.The recruitment reflects Orrick's broader strategy to stay ahead of regulatory developments and position itself as a leading adviser on technology-related risk for a changing legal landscape.Industry observers expect the appointment to enhance the firm's ability to help clients navigate complex rules as platforms face increasing scrutiny over practices affecting minors.

+ NICOLE LOPEZ JOINS ORRICK TO DRIVE YOUTH ONLINE SAFETY STRATEGY AND REGULATION

Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe announced the addition of Nicole Lopez, a recognized authority in youth online safety policy and litigation, to its team. Lopez previously held a senior role at Meta, underscoring her capability to navigate complex safety issues as the digital environment evolves.On Apr. 28, 2026, Lopez expressed enthusiasm for the new role and the opportunity to broaden her impact in the field.Her background spans policy, litigation and regulatory strategy, which the firm says will help clients facing increasing scrutiny over online safety. Jeremy Kudon, who leads Orrick's State Legislative practice, said, "We are facing a watershed moment. Online safety is top of mind for innovators, government, teens and parents, and society at large." The firm notes that 25 state legislatures are actively considering legislation aimed at safeguarding minors in digital spaces.Aravind Swaminathan, Orrick's Tech Sector Co-Leader, called Lopez a unique blend of skills—"part policy expert, part litigation and regulatory strategist, and part business advisor"—and said she will be an extraordinary resource for clients as AI reshapes the digital landscape. The Online Safety Law Center will leverage her leadership to keep clients informed of rapid developments and to advocate for policies that balance innovation with child protection.

urn:contentItem:6JJF-5SK3-S8FH-X15T-00000-00SC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-off2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:41ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSSC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-offNicole Blevins

When the wind is blowing in North Myrtle Beach, small-scale turbine blades spin in the breeze at three beach-access locations.

These turbines are not powering cities, spanning miles and standing hundreds of feet in the air. But they are part of South Carolina's plan to test wind energy potential, a plan that has been in the works for almost two decades.

Today South Carolina is home to one of the most advanced wind turbine testing facilities in the world and has years of mapping and feasibility studies under its belt, yet it is still one of six states in the U.S. without usable wind power generation.

In 2008, South Carolina Act 318 created a committee staffed by the state energy office to study and make recommendations for wind capability in the state. The study intended to understand the state's wind potential to determine its investment worth, and the committee submitted 18 recommendations to promote future projects.

Over the years, dozens of involved scientists, economic strategists and local governments joined to put motions into place by studying the economic opportunity, mapping sea floors and critical habitat and testing wind potential at different heights. Mainly funded by federal grants, the studies took hundreds of thousands of dollars, and researchers poured years of their time without knowing their progress would eventually be halted.

Dr. Paul Gayes, Coastal Carolina University executive director for the Burroughs and Chapin Center for marine and wetland studies, has spent his career studying renewable energies and the effects of carbon in the atmosphere. He's been part of the wind conversation from the start, but has seen other states jump on the opportunity before South Carolina.

"South Carolina wasn't slow necessarily," Gayes said. "It just takes forever to do things."

Now nearly 20 years later, the state sits with the same potential and no finish line in sight.

Cost of South Carolina energy and the need for renewables

Offshore wind energy started in the northeast where the resources and cost of energy were higher, but as technology has evolved, cost and resources in the southeast became less of an issue positioning the region for a better chance at success.

Gayes said initial investment for offshore wind energy can be costly, but onshore farms could be built tomorrow and be economically competitive.

Santee Cooper residential customers consumed over 2 million megawatt hours (MWh) in 2024 , and Dominion Energy residential customers consumed nearly 9 million MWh in 2025 . The average South Carolina residential customer paid $149.51 a month for power in 2024, and $155.14 in 2025, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

"We are an area that is growing at an enormously rapid rate," Gayes said. "The demand for power is going up."

In 2024, about half of South Carolina's energy generation came from nuclear plants, which don't release carbon or greenhouse gases, but nuclear is not a renewable energy source as it relies on uranium extraction and poses challenges with radioactive waste management. Only about 4% came from solar and hydrokinetic power, and the rest came from coal, natural gas and oil.

Considering the impact the state has already seen from a global carbon issue, wind energy has become a cost-effective way in other regions to reduce carbon emissions.

But the tug-of-war of political power and desire for renewable energy at the federal level has stunted progress nationwide, leaving South Carolina's potential for pay-off still up in the air.

History of wind energy in the state

The southeast is the last region in the country to develop any sort of wind energy dependence. It's mainly due to the lack of resources with the technology that was available. But South Carolina and nearby states started to play catch up in the 2000s during the country's push for renewable energy solutions.

One of the recommendations of a 2008 state feasibility study was to develop a Regulatory Task Force for Coastal Clean Energy, studying the potential for wind, wave and tidal energy development. Leaders of the task force wanted to know what a wind energy industry would look like for South Carolina's economy and if current regulations could foster an industry.

Initial modeling and reports suggested offshore wind farms could bring a significant amount of jobs, particularly with construction crews, engineers and mechanics.

In 2009, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded $45 million to Clemson University to develop a full-scale wind turbine testing facility using drive-train technology. Another $53 million of state and private donations led to its opening in 2013. It's now one of the most advanced turbine testing facilities in the world and serves a global market.

Clemson declined to be interviewed for the story.

Proponents of offshore wind pointed to the facility as an enormous asset for South Carolina's ability to transport the turbines due to its port location at the former U.S. Naval Base in North Charleston.

While recognition of offshore potential was slower statewide, one city welcomed the idea from the start.

North Myrtle Beach and Santee Cooper constructed a few small turbines, mainly under the guise of public awareness. The effort was to introduce locals to a technology they weren't familiar with and to educate about the economic benefits of wind.

Members of the North Strand Coastal Wind Team, Monroe Baldwin, former NMB Chamber of Commerce head of economic development; Greg Duckworth, former NMB councilman; and Gayes, partnered with the city to build an advocacy for wind energy in the area. Public outreach was a huge contributor to the support that was lacking in the rest of the state.

The City of North Myrtle Beach had a proclamation to become a wind powered city, Baldwin said, and he promoted the idea of using the turbines as a tourism opportunity for fishing charters, diving and sightseeing.

"North Myrtle Beach is the only coastal community that said 'Yes,'" Baldwin said. "Everybody else up and down the coast around us said, 'We don't want to see them.'"

Researchers continued the process of mapping areas of the state's coast and testing wind speeds, all steps that needed to occur before offshore wind leases could be granted. The Palmetto Wind Project, led by Gayes as an initial offshore study for Santee Cooper, found that winds get better as you go offshore and higher, and in the state they get better from south to north.

"Off the Grand Strand, there actually is, that was known at that time, a healthy potential for wind," Gayes said.

The area was also well-positioned for offshore leases because of the shallow depths of the area's outer continental shelf. If sea floors were deeper, offshore turbines would need high-scale floating systems to keep them stabilized, which cost an exorbitant amount to build. However, every mile further offshore raised the cost to build even traditional towers, Gayes said.

"While our wind fields were not necessarily stronger than it might be, say in Massachusetts or Maine, the cumulative effect of having a much bigger area that could be cheaply realized and the character of the winds is... I think South Carolina had the potential to be one of the top wind (production) states," Gayes said.

Since the Grand Strand area's power grid is relatively new, it could take a significant amount of energy from offshore without a major overhaul like it would in other parts of the country. Habitat mapping found minimal environmental impacts. There were no significant cultural resources or important shipwreck sites in the mapping area. Things appeared to be moving in a positive direction.

But since the offshore areas available to lease were in federal waters, they required federal action, and the administration handoff in 2016 changed the outlook for renewables across the country.

How federal politics interrupted state energy plans

In 2006, the Gulf of Mexico Energy Security Act created a moratorium banning oil and gas leasing in parts of the Gulf of Mexico and was set to expire on June 30, 2022.

But at the tail end of President Donald Trump's first administration, he extended the moratorium to ban offshore leasing in North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Florida for another decade, and included offshore wind leases.

When President Joe Biden's administration took over, efforts to revitalize investment in offshore wind escalated. The U.S. Department of Energy made a goal to reach 30 gigawatts of offshore wind energy production by 2030, kickstarting the leasing processes for other offshore projects in a more ready position. (One offshore wind turbine in future U.S. projects would produce between 12 and 14 megawatts, meaning there would have to be roughly 2,000 to 2,500 operational wind turbines to reach the goal).

While South Carolina was still getting its footing, offshore wind leases were granted in North Carolina in May 2022 in an attempt to secure them before the initial moratorium deadline. The Bureau of Ocean Energy Management auctioned the leases just across the border of the Grand Strand to TotalEnergies, a French energy company, and Duke Energy.

The Biden-Era Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 included a provision for every 60 million acres of oil and gas leasing put up for auction, offshore wind leasing must also be offered to create certainty for wind availability.

When administrations changed hands again, Trump doubled down on his stance against renewable energy, revoking the ability to lease offshore wind.

His latest action consisted of canceling offshore wind leases in New York and North Carolina in March 2026 and other projects in New Jersey, New York and California in April, essentially refunding the companies what they paid for the leases plus interest.

Karly Brownfield, senior program manager of the Southeastern Wind Coalition, said when the administration sends signals implying investors can follow all the rules to get a project permitted and approved but still face an extreme risk that a project could get stopped after significant investments have been made, it is not a signal an investor wants to see.

"At the end of the day, I think the biggest impact that the Trump administration has had on the offshore wind industry, (and) the clean energy industry as a whole, is uncertainty," she said.

While the lease canceling did not directly affect South Carolina, Gayes said because of the industry's halt the state won't see offshore production for likely another 15 years.

What could be next for South Carolina wind?

With offshore wind off the table for the time being, Brownfield recognized the state's opportunity to pivot to onshore wind investment.

Onshore wind farms are not typically seen in the southeast because of its presumptive lack of wind resources. But early models of wind potential were "tremendously different" than actual speeds, according to Gayes. Evolved turbine designs and taller towers can help accommodate the region, but transportation becomes an issue.

What's different about onshore projects in the southeast versus the Midwest or western regions is building wind farms on private land, Brownfield said. Building on private land, typically on someone's farm, offers economic benefits to rural communities.

"Because they're built in rural areas, the onshore wind farm, nine times out of 10 becomes the largest taxpayer in the county," she said.

In Chowan County, North Carolina, Timbermill Wind started construction in May 2023 and began clean energy generation in December 2024. It's projected to generate $50 million in tax revenue to the county in its 30-year lifespan. The landowners themselves also receive lease payments, providing a reliable source of income.

Brownfield said after the construction period, each tower takes up less than half an acre and still leaves significant usable land, but success of the projects depends on community support.

Gayes said he was working to put together proposals for a test bed site in South Carolina to prove the capacities and economics of onshore wind potential, but the change in administration, and with it interest in wind power, has put things on hold.

The lack of support for projects like onshore and offshore wind development often comes from a rejection of the world's climate issue caused by increased carbon output, Gayes said.

As a coastal state already seeing the effects of a warming climate - increased inland flooding, beach erosion and sea-level rise - Gayes said doing nothing is not an option.

The smarter option would be to invest in all types of energy production, including oil and gas, but at varying degrees, Gayes said, but the reality of transforming an oil-based economy overnight is impractical.

"There is no magic bullet," he said.

While the future of wind in South Carolina is uncertain, the preparation is already there for when the time comes for offshore leasing, and Gayes is certain it will come.

urn:contentItem:6JJP-F6J3-RSGF-3031-00000-00ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION - CURRENT DEVELOPMENTS IN THE SECTOR AND PEERS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:37ZLexisNexisSTOCKROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION - CURRENT DEVELOPMENTS IN THE SECTOR AND PEERS

DASHBOARD: ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION

PermID4296528943Websitehttps://www.rockefellerfoundation.org/IndustryPhilanthropy / Global DevelopmentAddress420 Fifth Avenue, New York City, New York, U.S.

ACTIVITIES:

The Rockefeller Foundation, founded in 1913 by John D. Rockefeller, is a global philanthropy headquartered in New York City. With around 200 staff, it manages a $6 billion endowment, granting $300 million annually to advance health, equity, and sustainability. In 2024, it committed $100 million to climate resilience in African cities. Its culture emphasizes innovation, collaboration, and data-driven impact, with training in global policy and grantmaking. Competing with Ford Foundation, Rockefeller stands out for its pioneering role in public health and green energy, including founding the Global Energy Alliance for People and Planet. Recent initiatives include AI for social good and support for the National Museum of African American History, aligning with social justice trends. The foundations century-long legacy includes eradicating hookworm and funding the Green Revolution, shaping modern philanthropy.

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION [3 News That Matters]

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION CELEBRATES 60 YEARS IN NAIROBI, SIGNALS AFRICA-CENTERED DEVELOPMENT PUSH

NAIROBI — The Rockefeller Foundation marked the 60th anniversary of its Africa Regional Office, established in Nairobi, Kenya, in 1966, on the 1st of May, 2026. The milestone was celebrated as part of AfricaXchange 2026, a forum to catalyze systemic reform by uniting leaders who can mobilize capital, influence policy discussions, and scale viable solutions to Africa’s challenges. The anniversary event drew more than 400 leaders from business, government, and civil society, underscoring the Foundation’s century-long work in human capital, health, agriculture, and energy across the continent.William Asiko, Senior Vice President and head of the Africa Regional Office, noted the changing landscape of development finance: "African philanthropy, private capital, and regional institutions are reshaping not just how development is financed, but how it is delivered." His remarks came as OECD data showed a decline in official development assistance, reinforcing the need for new investment models and stronger partnerships that position Africa as an equal partner in shaping its economic future.In the closing session, Elizabeth Yee, Executive Vice President of Programs, insisted that philanthropy’s job is not to be the hero: "It’s to take risk others can’t, build trust where it’s been missing, and move countries from ambition to execution." The conference theme, "Money, Markets, and Mindsets," reflected the aim of financing and leading Africa with African voices. The Rockefeller Foundation also launched the inaugural Africa Big Bets Fellows, a class of ten changemakers advancing community-led solutions across several countries. The event highlighted cross-sector collaboration through participants such as E. Prof. Anyang’ Nyong’o, Governor of Kisumu County, and E. Nardos Bekele-Thomas, CEO of the African Union Development Agency.The Foundation’s long relationship with Africa predates the Nairobi office, tracing back to early 20th-century initiatives including the 1925 West Africa Yellow Fever Commission. Today, roughly one-third of The Rockefeller Foundation’s global funding supports Africa, with priorities spanning energy abundance, food security, health, and innovation. By emphasizing local solutions and strengthened partnerships, the Rockefeller Foundation aims to advance a sustainable, prosperous future for the continent.

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION LAUNCHES AFRICA BIG BETS FELLOWS TO ACCELERATE LOCAL SOLUTIONS

The Rockefeller Foundation unveiled the inaugural Africa Big Bets Fellows during its AfricaXchange convening in Nairobi, Kenya. Ten changemakers from Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Nigeria, South Africa, and Tanzania were selected to lead community-driven solutions to Africa's development challenges. The five-month fellowship aims to scale high-impact ideas in areas such as energy access, food security, healthcare, financial inclusion, climate resilience, and migration.The launch coincides with 28th of April, 2026, marking the 60th anniversary of the Rockefeller Foundation's Africa Regional Office and underscoring its long-standing engagement on the continent. Foundation leaders described the fellows as a new generation of African innovators whose locally driven solutions are designed to expand opportunity, strengthen resilience, and raise living standards across health, agriculture, clean energy, and digital finance.Across Africa, the program arrives amid persistent challenges such as limited electricity access for hundreds of millions and gaps in global climate finance, but it also highlights Africa's vast potential for renewable energy and sustainable growth. The fellows' projects include mobile healthcare in rural Ghana, waste-to-resource systems for cleaner cities, regenerative agriculture linked to school feeding in Kenya, AI-powered farming in Malawi, and expanding access to financial services in Nigeria, among others.

+ ROCKEFELLER FOUNDATION COMMITS TO WORKFORCE RESILIENCE AMID AI DISRUPTION

On 28th April 2026, the Rockefeller Foundation announced a grant-based national initiative to address disruptions from automation, aiming to prevent job displacement and help workers transition into AI-complementary roles. The program prioritizes workforce interventions over product development, stressing program design, metrics and evaluation. It will measure displacement risk, deployment outcomes and labor-market transitions to understand how automation affects employment. Funding will come through grants, programmatic pilots and partnerships with employers, training providers and local governments. The choices made during implementation will be critical in determining whether the foundation's funding yields scalable reskilling pipelines or merely funds isolated projects. This initiative is a strategic philanthropic signal rather than a regulatory or corporate move, intended to catalyze innovative retraining models and public-private collaboration, complementing ongoing discussions about workforce policies such as sectoral training and apprenticeships. It is also expected to boost demand for measurable, data-driven evaluations within the skills and labor analytics ecosystem.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ FORD FOUNDATION [3 News That Matters]

+ FORD FOUNDATION LAUNCHES $10 MILLION HOMETOWN FUND TO REVITALIZE ROSEBURG

ROSEBURG, Ore. – The Ford Foundation is launching the Hometown Fund, a $10 million investment aimed at revitalizing Roseburg over the next decade. The date of this announcement is 5th May 2026. The program will support large-scale, community-driven capital projects within the city limits, with capital grants of up to $1 million and pre-development planning requests of up to $75,000. The foundation seeks to unite private and public partners to drive transformative change in the community."So I really, really hope that the grown-ups can work together to build tangible spaces and places where kids can say that they are loved and that there’s a future here," said Kara Inae Carlisle, President and CEO of the Ford Foundation. She underscored the goal of meaningful, long-term improvement over the next decade and a commitment to fostering environments where families can thrive. This local initiative sits within the foundation's broader charitable giving, which has totaled about $810 million since its inception.Historically, the Ford Foundation has focused on education, early childhood development, and community economic development. Its flagship Ford Scholars Program currently supports around 1,000 students annually, providing resources to pursue academic goals. The foundation has also played a critical role in regional crisis recovery, partnering with the Oregon Community Foundation and Meyer Memorial Trust to distribute $10 million for rebuilding after the 2020 Labor Day wildfires.Organizations interested in applying for the Hometown Fund must start by completing an online information form outlining their project’s scope and anticipated community impact. After the initial submission, Ford staff will review the proposal and reach out to schedule a meeting with the applicant. Projects aligned with multi-sector collaboration and large-scale transformation will receive an invitation to submit a formal application.The Hometown Fund represents a significant commitment by the Ford Foundation to Roseburg’s future, aiming to create lasting change for current residents and future generations.

+ NHRC RECOGNIZES FORD FOUNDATION FOR SUSTAINED HUMAN RIGHTS SUPPORT IN NIGERIA

On Apr. 28, 2026, the National Human Rights Commission in Abuja awarded the Ford Foundation an Award of Appreciation for its sustained contributions to human rights in Nigeria. The honor was collected by ChiChi Aniagolu-Okoye, the Foundation's Regional Director for West Africa, during a gathering with NHRC senior staff and officials.NHRC Executive Secretary Dr. Tony Ojukwu said the Foundation has consistently supported efforts to widen access to justice and to boost the Commission's capacity. He noted that Nigeria faces complex rights challenges and that ongoing collaboration with development partners is essential to turning policy into practical improvements for citizens. The reception underscored the importance of continuing joint work, with gratitude also extended by Hilary Ogbonna, the Commission's Senior Human Rights Adviser, who highlighted milestones such as the MOVE project and the dashboard system that benefited from the Ford Foundation's early backing.

+ FORD FOUNDATION URGES ECOWAS FIRST LADIES TO TARGET GBV THROUGH SOFT POWER

The Ford Foundation has urged ECOWAS First Ladies to deploy their influence more strategically in tackling gender-based violence, describing the First Ladies’ soft power as a critical driver of social change and institutional accountability. The appeal was made by ChiChi Aniagolu, West Africa Regional Director of the Ford Foundation, during a goodwill message at the ECOWAS First Ladies Forum on Zero Tolerance for GBV, held in Banjul under the SWEDD+ platform in partnership with the World Bank and the United Nations Population Fund on 24th April 2026.Aniagolu warned that despite existing frameworks and commitments, gender-based violence remains widespread, with one in three women globally affected. In West and Central Africa, harmful practices such as child marriage persist, with over 30 percent of girls in some countries married before the age of 18. She also highlighted the economic burden of GBV, estimating losses of up to 3.7 percent of GDP in affected countries — figures that exceed investments in key sectors such as education.While the Ford Foundation acknowledged that First Ladies are not formal policymakers, it stressed their significant role in shaping public discourse and influencing decision-making across the continent. Aniagolu cited examples of impactful First Ladies, including Maryam Babangida, whose Better Life for Rural Women Programme elevated rural women's issues into national discussions, and Fatima Bio, whose Hands Off Our Girls campaign contributed to a national emergency declaration on rape in 2019. Such instances illustrate how soft power, when utilized effectively, can instigate systemic change.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JJF-5SK3-S8FH-X16K-00000-00SC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-off2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:41ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSSC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-offNicole Blevins

When the wind is blowing in North Myrtle Beach, small-scale turbine blades spin in the breeze at three beach-access locations.

These turbines are not powering cities, spanning miles and standing hundreds of feet in the air. But they are part of South Carolina's plan to test wind energy potential, a plan that has been in the works for almost two decades.

Today South Carolina is home to one of the most advanced wind turbine testing facilities in the world and has years of mapping and feasibility studies under its belt, yet it is still one of six states in the U.S. without usable wind power generation.

In 2008, South Carolina Act 318 created a committee staffed by the state energy office to study and make recommendations for wind capability in the state. The study intended to understand the state's wind potential to determine its investment worth, and the committee submitted 18 recommendations to promote future projects.

Over the years, dozens of involved scientists, economic strategists and local governments joined to put motions into place by studying the economic opportunity, mapping sea floors and critical habitat and testing wind potential at different heights. Mainly funded by federal grants, the studies took hundreds of thousands of dollars, and researchers poured years of their time without knowing their progress would eventually be halted.

Dr. Paul Gayes, Coastal Carolina University executive director for the Burroughs and Chapin Center for marine and wetland studies, has spent his career studying renewable energies and the effects of carbon in the atmosphere. He's been part of the wind conversation from the start, but has seen other states jump on the opportunity before South Carolina.

"South Carolina wasn't slow necessarily," Gayes said. "It just takes forever to do things."

Now nearly 20 years later, the state sits with the same potential and no finish line in sight.

Cost of South Carolina energy and the need for renewables

Offshore wind energy started in the northeast where the resources and cost of energy were higher, but as technology has evolved, cost and resources in the southeast became less of an issue positioning the region for a better chance at success.

Gayes said initial investment for offshore wind energy can be costly, but onshore farms could be built tomorrow and be economically competitive.

Santee Cooper residential customers consumed over 2 million megawatt hours (MWh) in 2024 , and Dominion Energy residential customers consumed nearly 9 million MWh in 2025 . The average South Carolina residential customer paid $149.51 a month for power in 2024, and $155.14 in 2025, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

"We are an area that is growing at an enormously rapid rate," Gayes said. "The demand for power is going up."

In 2024, about half of South Carolina's energy generation came from nuclear plants, which don't release carbon or greenhouse gases, but nuclear is not a renewable energy source as it relies on uranium extraction and poses challenges with radioactive waste management. Only about 4% came from solar and hydrokinetic power, and the rest came from coal, natural gas and oil.

Considering the impact the state has already seen from a global carbon issue, wind energy has become a cost-effective way in other regions to reduce carbon emissions.

But the tug-of-war of political power and desire for renewable energy at the federal level has stunted progress nationwide, leaving South Carolina's potential for pay-off still up in the air.

History of wind energy in the state

The southeast is the last region in the country to develop any sort of wind energy dependence. It's mainly due to the lack of resources with the technology that was available. But South Carolina and nearby states started to play catch up in the 2000s during the country's push for renewable energy solutions.

One of the recommendations of a 2008 state feasibility study was to develop a Regulatory Task Force for Coastal Clean Energy, studying the potential for wind, wave and tidal energy development. Leaders of the task force wanted to know what a wind energy industry would look like for South Carolina's economy and if current regulations could foster an industry.

Initial modeling and reports suggested offshore wind farms could bring a significant amount of jobs, particularly with construction crews, engineers and mechanics.

In 2009, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded $45 million to Clemson University to develop a full-scale wind turbine testing facility using drive-train technology. Another $53 million of state and private donations led to its opening in 2013. It's now one of the most advanced turbine testing facilities in the world and serves a global market.

Clemson declined to be interviewed for the story.

Proponents of offshore wind pointed to the facility as an enormous asset for South Carolina's ability to transport the turbines due to its port location at the former U.S. Naval Base in North Charleston.

While recognition of offshore potential was slower statewide, one city welcomed the idea from the start.

North Myrtle Beach and Santee Cooper constructed a few small turbines, mainly under the guise of public awareness. The effort was to introduce locals to a technology they weren't familiar with and to educate about the economic benefits of wind.

Members of the North Strand Coastal Wind Team, Monroe Baldwin, former NMB Chamber of Commerce head of economic development; Greg Duckworth, former NMB councilman; and Gayes, partnered with the city to build an advocacy for wind energy in the area. Public outreach was a huge contributor to the support that was lacking in the rest of the state.

The City of North Myrtle Beach had a proclamation to become a wind powered city, Baldwin said, and he promoted the idea of using the turbines as a tourism opportunity for fishing charters, diving and sightseeing.

"North Myrtle Beach is the only coastal community that said 'Yes,'" Baldwin said. "Everybody else up and down the coast around us said, 'We don't want to see them.'"

Researchers continued the process of mapping areas of the state's coast and testing wind speeds, all steps that needed to occur before offshore wind leases could be granted. The Palmetto Wind Project, led by Gayes as an initial offshore study for Santee Cooper, found that winds get better as you go offshore and higher, and in the state they get better from south to north.

"Off the Grand Strand, there actually is, that was known at that time, a healthy potential for wind," Gayes said.

The area was also well-positioned for offshore leases because of the shallow depths of the area's outer continental shelf. If sea floors were deeper, offshore turbines would need high-scale floating systems to keep them stabilized, which cost an exorbitant amount to build. However, every mile further offshore raised the cost to build even traditional towers, Gayes said.

"While our wind fields were not necessarily stronger than it might be, say in Massachusetts or Maine, the cumulative effect of having a much bigger area that could be cheaply realized and the character of the winds is... I think South Carolina had the potential to be one of the top wind (production) states," Gayes said.

Since the Grand Strand area's power grid is relatively new, it could take a significant amount of energy from offshore without a major overhaul like it would in other parts of the country. Habitat mapping found minimal environmental impacts. There were no significant cultural resources or important shipwreck sites in the mapping area. Things appeared to be moving in a positive direction.

But since the offshore areas available to lease were in federal waters, they required federal action, and the administration handoff in 2016 changed the outlook for renewables across the country.

How federal politics interrupted state energy plans

In 2006, the Gulf of Mexico Energy Security Act created a moratorium banning oil and gas leasing in parts of the Gulf of Mexico and was set to expire on June 30, 2022.

But at the tail end of President Donald Trump's first administration, he extended the moratorium to ban offshore leasing in North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Florida for another decade, and included offshore wind leases.

When President Joe Biden's administration took over, efforts to revitalize investment in offshore wind escalated. The U.S. Department of Energy made a goal to reach 30 gigawatts of offshore wind energy production by 2030, kickstarting the leasing processes for other offshore projects in a more ready position. (One offshore wind turbine in future U.S. projects would produce between 12 and 14 megawatts, meaning there would have to be roughly 2,000 to 2,500 operational wind turbines to reach the goal).

While South Carolina was still getting its footing, offshore wind leases were granted in North Carolina in May 2022 in an attempt to secure them before the initial moratorium deadline. The Bureau of Ocean Energy Management auctioned the leases just across the border of the Grand Strand to TotalEnergies, a French energy company, and Duke Energy.

The Biden-Era Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 included a provision for every 60 million acres of oil and gas leasing put up for auction, offshore wind leasing must also be offered to create certainty for wind availability.

When administrations changed hands again, Trump doubled down on his stance against renewable energy, revoking the ability to lease offshore wind.

His latest action consisted of canceling offshore wind leases in New York and North Carolina in March 2026 and other projects in New Jersey, New York and California in April, essentially refunding the companies what they paid for the leases plus interest.

Karly Brownfield, senior program manager of the Southeastern Wind Coalition, said when the administration sends signals implying investors can follow all the rules to get a project permitted and approved but still face an extreme risk that a project could get stopped after significant investments have been made, it is not a signal an investor wants to see.

"At the end of the day, I think the biggest impact that the Trump administration has had on the offshore wind industry, (and) the clean energy industry as a whole, is uncertainty," she said.

While the lease canceling did not directly affect South Carolina, Gayes said because of the industry's halt the state won't see offshore production for likely another 15 years.

What could be next for South Carolina wind?

With offshore wind off the table for the time being, Brownfield recognized the state's opportunity to pivot to onshore wind investment.

Onshore wind farms are not typically seen in the southeast because of its presumptive lack of wind resources. But early models of wind potential were "tremendously different" than actual speeds, according to Gayes. Evolved turbine designs and taller towers can help accommodate the region, but transportation becomes an issue.

What's different about onshore projects in the southeast versus the Midwest or western regions is building wind farms on private land, Brownfield said. Building on private land, typically on someone's farm, offers economic benefits to rural communities.

"Because they're built in rural areas, the onshore wind farm, nine times out of 10 becomes the largest taxpayer in the county," she said.

In Chowan County, North Carolina, Timbermill Wind started construction in May 2023 and began clean energy generation in December 2024. It's projected to generate $50 million in tax revenue to the county in its 30-year lifespan. The landowners themselves also receive lease payments, providing a reliable source of income.

Brownfield said after the construction period, each tower takes up less than half an acre and still leaves significant usable land, but success of the projects depends on community support.

Gayes said he was working to put together proposals for a test bed site in South Carolina to prove the capacities and economics of onshore wind potential, but the change in administration, and with it interest in wind power, has put things on hold.

The lack of support for projects like onshore and offshore wind development often comes from a rejection of the world's climate issue caused by increased carbon output, Gayes said.

As a coastal state already seeing the effects of a warming climate - increased inland flooding, beach erosion and sea-level rise - Gayes said doing nothing is not an option.

The smarter option would be to invest in all types of energy production, including oil and gas, but at varying degrees, Gayes said, but the reality of transforming an oil-based economy overnight is impractical.

"There is no magic bullet," he said.

While the future of wind in South Carolina is uncertain, the preparation is already there for when the time comes for offshore leasing, and Gayes is certain it will come.
